speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, reel six, with Alex Reifer. Reel six. I kind of cut you off at the end of the-- at reel five. And I apologize. I guess what I wanted to know was how you actually found out you lost your family through the Red Cross, through the Joint? How did you, actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find out between people. People which were in concentration camp. When I came to Sosnowiec, they told me that my whole family perished in Auschwitz. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They took him to walk to Birkenau, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop for a second.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead, I'm sorry. You-- you said when you were in Sosnowiec, you talked to people who had seen your family perish?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the-- when I was in Sosnowiec and I talked to people and they didn't see perish. They know when they was-- when they took them to-- to-- to the crematorium. And when they take the crematorium people, they forgot to come out. And this is the way it was that I know that my family perished in Auschwitz, in concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it-- did you have any relatives left? Cousins?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have-- I have family here. I have cousins. Which I have my uncle's son, Heinz Reifer. But they came into the United States a long time ago. They came from Vienna. He was living in Vienna. From Vienna they came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- before, yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before, when Hitler-- when Hitler took Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah, in the [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they left. They closed-- they had got the bank in Austria, in Vienna, very big. He just passed away not long ago. He was a-- the youngest brother from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- so I need to know what happened to you next, after? Did you-- were you discharged? Did you leave the army-- how did you get out of the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec, when I finished the four weeks, which are supposed to be vacation, I left again to-- to Moscow. And when I left to Moscow, they send me back to-- to-- to Berlin. And from Berlin, again, they-- after a short time, they send me back to Moscow. Because they couldn't release me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- because I was in reconnaissance. I was a top-- like, a top man in the FBI, or something like that. You understand? You don't release people like that. You don't throw them in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in-- in Moscow, I told-- in the main office, and they have got respect for me. I told them you know what? Transfer me to the Polish army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which they did. I got a consulate to the Polish army. But I came into Auschwitz. And I forgot to call the Polish army. I forgot. And I-- and I pay people money. And they took me to Antwerp, to Belgium. And that's all, what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Antwerp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was five years in Antwerp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I do-- normally, when I came to Antwerp-- this is also a good question. I am good with the hands. And also, I was when I was a kid. Then I met a person. The name is [? Oskar Feldenstein. ?] And [? Oskar Feldenstein ?] has got a restaurant. And he make parties. And his restaurant, it was in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the diamond [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] means a whole building. But he has got a restaurant down there. And he made a lot of parties.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I said, I am like you say, culinary art. He said, you know what? I will take you to one party.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if you think you can do the same or better, you have a big job. I said, good. He took me to one party. I said, this is nothing. I can make anything you-- much better. And from now, from this time, I was working for him. I make ice carvings. I make centerpieces. I make-- he-- I build up his business, unbelievable. And also, I was doing some business. Because they find out what I am doing. It's a big-- one of the biggest restaurants in Antwerp. It was-- it was the Atlas Company. And I made for him too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about all your medals?

speaker: interviewee
text: The medals? [LAUGHS] Not only the medals, the medals and uniforms. I got several uniforms, everything make to me, to order. You know, I was-- [LAUGHS] and before I left from-- from Belgium to the United States, in 1952, I was living in a-- in a small apartment. And I don't want to bring anything with me. Then I-- I got as big a chair as that. I opened up the back. I take everything out from the back. I put all, everything in. And I sew it back. And I have everything in the chair there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this chair is today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know where it is today. But if I go to Antwerp, I will try to buy the whole chair. Even if they want $5,000, I'll pay them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how can you find-- [LAUGHS] But how can you find the chair?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I-- if the chair is still in this particular apartment, I can find it. Because nobody will look underneath what's going on. It's a heavy chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't want--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I have all the medals. I can get the medals back when I go to San Francisco Embassy. Because they have-- all the medals, they have-- they have writing in-- in-- in Moscow, all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is the Polish or Soviet-- is the Polish or the Russian army looking for you, after you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? No, no, no. Nobody look for me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what made you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to-- to-- to Belgium, and I wrote to my uncle that I'm here, in Belgium. And he flew in. He came to me. And he said I will bring you over to-- to New York. And he was working on papers. And I got the uncle-- the mother's brother-- name Feiner. And he has got a tremendous big company in New York. The company is name was Tip-Top Ash can company. He makes all the ashcans from all over-- from the United States was in his-- a big company. And he made me the papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you arrived, what-- do you know the exact date that you arrived in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written down. But I do not exactly remember the date, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel, when you-- did you come on-- by ship or by plane?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came by ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you feel when you saw the Statue of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantastic. And I came to the United States. My two uncles was waiting for me. And they took me to a hotel. And I was staying down in a hotel. They visit me. They took me out. They invite me-- this uncle invite me for-- for a dinner party. And the other uncle invite me for a dinner party. They bring-- they came in. They took me down there. And that's all. And that's all. That's all, what it was, with my family. And they introduced me to the children. And I met all-- all the family, slowly. The only problem was, with me, that I didn't speak English in this particular time. But slowly I learned, I learned, I learned. And now I speak a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do for a-- what-- what have you done for a living since you arrived in the United-- what-- what do you do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did? I can tell you what I did. My-- I have a cousin here. He passed away. And the name was Rabbi Ekstein. He was in concentration camp. And my-- my cousin, his wife, Esther Ekstein-- Etia Ekstein. And they was also in concentration camp. And they open up-- and he came to New York. They opened up a fish market. And I visit him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he told me, Alex, you-- you want to do something? I said, yeah. What do you want to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I-- if you have some caterers which you supply the fish, I can try to work with them. And then they have got a caterer. The name-- it still is-- Nathan Trotsky Catering in New York, a very big catering company.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took me to Nathan Trotsky. He-- he interview me. And he said, OK. Tomorrow I have a party. Can you come to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yeah. And I make him a party. And the first party was a small 150 people. And he hired me. And I was making parties for Waldorf Astoria, Essex House, [INAUDIBLE]. I-- I was recognized as a good culinary artist.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Los Angeles? I came a little bit later. I don't know what year I came to Los Angeles. I came-- I came to Los Angeles after I married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1963.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's name is Susana Reifer. She-- her name was Susana Tuber. And my wife, my wife was born in 1934. And she was born in Buenos Aires, Argentina. And when I visit my aunt in Santiago, Chile, my wife was working for my aunt as private secretary. And this I met her. And we were-- start writing. And I learn Spanish. And we got married in 1963, in my uncle's hotel, hotel New Yorker. And he gave me three floors for people. I invite a lot of people from all the countries. And we didn't charge them for the rooms. And-- and I got a beautiful wedding. Fantastic wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two. Anna Marey, and Brian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you name them for anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Brian is the name after my father, Berl, Brian. Anna Marey is the name from two mothers, my mother, Miriam, and my wife's mother, Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of business are you in today?

speaker: interviewee
text: We manufacture, already, a long time, 14-carat gold jewelry. And we sell to-- we don't sell to-- to public. We only sell to big companies, very big companies. And we cast. We do everything. We buy only-- which we buy is 24-carat gold. And we make, from the 24-carat gold, we make 14-carat gold. And we-- I design. And my wife is also a designer. And we have, now, normally around between 4,000 and 6,000 molds for casting. We cast every day. We cast. We polish. We set the diamonds. And we ship out the merchandise. And we do very, very, very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What I will have?

speaker: interviewer
text: What should I tell her? And I have one last question for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is now that it's over 50 years since the war is over, what is it that you want to pass on to your children, and eventually your grandchildren, about what happened to you and to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will explain you something. I start talking to my children, my [NON-ENGLISH], you don't want to listen. You don't want to hear about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't talk to them about it. But I assure you, if I will have grandchildren, which I will tell him, when they are younger, maybe, the interest. See? Because my background, not too many people pass through what I did in a short time. I mean, I called it a short time. See? But what do you want them to remember most? What do you want to pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I-- I want to-- I want to pass what I saw in the world, how-- how people hate people, how people like people. You see, when you-- when you teach somebody, a person, you must teach them both sides. You couldn't teach them only straight, one side. You must show 'em. I-- I can tell people what kind of people in the world hate people and what kind of people in the world like people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think that concludes this portion of our interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What we-- I think we're going to do the photographs first. That the set of photographs, which are going to be many, are the photographs that Mr. Reifer took from the SS officer from the 16th panzer division.

speaker: interviewee
text: 16th panzer division. 16th panzer division.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Panzer means-- panzer means-- how do you call it in English?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tank.

speaker: interviewee
text: A tank Is a panzer. A tank is a panzer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that they-- the photos will be very graphic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the pictures which you will see I took from a Captain, from the 16 panzer division SS, in. 1943

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this? What is this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo from the Zyklon. And the Zyklon is the gas which goes in for the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from Brausebad. Brausebad means the people going inside. And they think that they get a shower. But you don't get a shower. They get a shower with gas. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is a picture when the people came out from the Brausebad. And the preparation is for the gas chamber, to burn these people. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This is a picture which is marked down on the top, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' work makes you free. I think this is free of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is, of course, the entrance to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The entrance to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Auschwitz. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know what this is. This looks like some kind of digging that's going on or building. You can talk. Oh, I think you can actually see-- you can talk. You can actually see it better on the screen than you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nm-mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He doesn't recognize.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't we hand it to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give me this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the concentration camp. The people which are-- before they get to the brausebad and crematorium. They lived down there in the meantime.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is-- make sure you always say this is in Auschwitz, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- also where the people live in the meantime, before they get cremated or brausebad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the German officer whom you interrogated, did he tell you anything about these photographs at all? Did he go-- OK. You need to say yes or no. I can't--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead. You don't know what this is?

speaker: interviewer
text: This looks like the interior of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the entrance to the concentration camp, in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: You should tell us what that says.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the eyeglasses from the people, which they took before they send them to concentration camp, or to-- or to the brausebad, and to the crematorium. When they cremated the people, before they cremated, they took off the eyeglasses. And this is in Oswiecim. And the Germans called it Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's something else written near the bottom, underneath Oswiecim. Can you read what that is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the stack of the eyeglasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'll get closer in a second. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: He can't read it though. That's the problem. We need to get closer.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm right on top of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you read that, Mr. Reifer? Oh, he's thinking. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from a person which is already dying. And it is marked down Oswiecim. That means, in German, Auschwitz. And they died truly for-- oswobodzeniu. Oswobodzeniu means to-- to relieve himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning that death was a relief.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one of the blocks in concentration camp. And the block is number 10, 11. It's two blocks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Go ahead, sir.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also Oswiecim, Auschwitz. And this is the barracks, how the people lived inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, you can really see the-- you can really see what's in them, can't you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, I can't see anything from here. But I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- this is pictures from dead people which was strewing out from the brausebad. And they take them to the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: These people is also taking out from the brausebad and prepared the people are-- they prepare the-- people for the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crematorium, to burn.

speaker: interviewer
text: There are obviously German officers, German soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sight is German soldiers, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This picture is also the-- from the brausebad, and prepared for the crematorium. In the crematorium they burned people for-- to ashes. This is the crematorium. A gas chamber is the same as brausebad. You don't have time to do it in the gas chamber, then they do it. And they-- they tell them, take off your clothes. Go in and take a shower. And instead of the shower, they put in gas. That means the preparation for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's also in Auschwitz. Also, people who are taken out from the brausebad, and prepare for the crematorium. These people, which are holding a skeleton on the floor, these people are the same people which they will be dead, maybe a week or a day later. This is organizers, which they're in charge of the taking away the corpses. And this is also the same type of people which will be dead in a-- in a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This shows the oven. The oven where they burned the people. He showed how they-- how they put in the dead people in the oven. They push them in and they close. And they burn them. And they make ashes from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is also people which they're loading on a truck. This is taking out from the brausebad to the crematorium. And the people which are helping are the same people which will be dead in a couple-- in a short time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the look-- how it looks in the concentration camp. It's all wired, all around, from both sides. And this is in Auschwitz, in Oswiecim.

speaker: interviewer
text: I put that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead. Go ahead. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Reifer, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from the German soldiers staying in the front-- it must be a-- a big office, from big officers, which you're seeing in the front of it. In Oswiecim, Auschwitz. Picture shows-- normally it start with the-- the picture, number 39. This is a picture, also, from-- from the people which was out from the brausebad, they're calling it. Brausebad means a shower but in Oswiecim, Auschwitz. And these people is preparing these people for the crematorium, to burn them. And they're waiting for a truck to put them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this picture shows, also, the same thing from the brausebad, tooking out the people. And-- and in the front is, you see, German soldiers with-- with-- with carbines, having making the orders down there. In Oswiecim, Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows a person which is prepared to send a man to the brausebad. He wouldn't come out any more. They will take him out. And this is in Auschwitz, Oswiecim.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish is [SPEAKING POLISH], children in the concentration camp in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman showed also to prepare the-- she is prepared to go to--

speaker: interviewer
text: This looks like this woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the brausebad.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I also wonder if perhaps, which I don't know for sure, but there's something written on the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Something is written on the side. I don't--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if this woman was-- had been forced into any sort of experimentation. Look at the size of her breast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the size of the breast is terrible. One side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Now, what I'm thinking is perhaps--

speaker: interviewee
text: It can be. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe we can turn the picture over.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Oswiecim,' this is Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's really hard to read.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very hard to read.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'She is prepared to go to work.'

speaker: interviewer
text: To work? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [POLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] Do I don't know, Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you get the writing though, in it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I'm right on top of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau, Brzezinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: With--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this must be the same woman. And we think that the-- she-- she is prepared for some kind of a preparation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Operation, experiment, who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: Preparation, and experiments. Again, it's Oswiecim.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait. Wait. Wait. Wait. Wait. Wait.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. Read.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oswiecim is Auschwitz. And this is-- [POLISH], it means it goes out to work, in the city of Birkenau. She goes-- this way she goes to work, to Birkenau. This picture is from my grandfather. His name is Meyer Reifer. And he lived in Oswiecim, in Poland. The Germans called it Auschwitz. And he was a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- this photograph was taken around 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture from my family. The name is Reifer, family, Reifer. And my name is Alex Reifer. They call me, in Oswiecim, Iser. And I was born in 1921, in Oswiecim, February 7.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- you want to start from right to left or left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I start right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I go to left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, OK. Just go--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I go to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: --right ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go right ahead and tell us who everybody is. Their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: Second to me is my father. His name is Berl Reifer. The third is my mother, is Miriam Reifer. And the fourth is my brother, Leopold Reifer. He was born in 1923. The forth is-- the name is [INAUDIBLE]. In Polish, you call him [INAUDIBLE]. And he was born, I think, in 1925. And the middle is my youngest brother. The name is Hersh Joseph. He was-- his name was made-- given after my mother's father passed away. Hersh Joseph Feiner. The fifth brother is Chaim. They call him Chaim Reifer. All of them are perished, except me. I'm still alive. Perished in Auschwitz, Oswiecim, during the war. I think it was in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you also tell me what year this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in 1934, when I was bar mitzvah, 13 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Oswiecim, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture shows I am in the middle. From my right is from-- where I'm sitting here now, from my right. I'm the fourth. I am in the fourth, in the Russian uniform. And I was the first officer which I met the 82nd Division and grab off Ludwigslust [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And May 4, 19-- 90-- 1945, in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
